title: Observable — How Atoms Share Electrons

shots.speech_text: Why does water climb up a straw, dissolve salt, and stay liquid across a huge temperature range — when a similar-sized molecule would be a gas?

shots.video_prompt: CONTINUITY LOCK @Julian-001: identical presenter face, smart-casual wardrobe, seamless neutral set, same eye-line to camera — seamless continuation of prior take, zero jump cut. Presenter explains the phenomenon plainly, motivated gesture, clinical lighting. Camera: slow push-in medium. Neutral seamless 5500K bilateral soft key locked — symmetrical wrap, subtle nose shadow only, zero hue drift, no magenta vignette, no warm/cool lateral split. Neutral 5600–6500K even field — shadow definition for shape only, no gel, no warm practicals in frame. Locked-off or slow linear track; no handheld. Lip-synced, delivers: "Atoms with nearly equal hunger for electrons can share them instead of handing them over completely. That shared pair is a covalent bond. In water, oxygen shares with two hydrogens, but not equally — oxygen pulls harder, so the molecule is bent and polar." clear enthusiastic science communicator voice, precise but accessible diction, synthetic presenter only. Finish on presentational gesture peak, never hold dead stillness or dead frame.
shots.speech_text: Atoms with nearly equal hunger for electrons can share them instead of handing them over completely. That shared pair is a covalent bond. In water, oxygen shares with two hydrogens, but not equally — oxygen pulls harder, so the molecule is bent and polar.
